solutions.solution:
Thus, the simple rule to derive a conclusion from a combined inequality is: make the common term disappear.
Ex. 5: Derive a conclusion from the following combined inequality:
(i) x > y > z
Soln: (i) x > z
(ii) x < z
Ex. 6: However, when we have '≥' signs in the combined inequality, the process is a bit more thought-provoking. For example, consider the following combined inequality.
x ≥ y ≥ z
Here, x is either greater than y or equal to y. Therefore, the minimum case for x is equal to y.
But 'y' is always greater than z. (because even when x attains its least value it is equal to y which is always greater than z). Therefore, x will always be greater than z). Hence, the inequality as conclusion is:
x > z

Ex. 7: Now, consider the combined inequality
x > y ≥ z
Here, x is always greater than y and y is either greater than z or equal to z.
If 'When y is greater than z, x will obviously be greater than z. Even when y is equal to z, x will be greater z because x is always greater than y.
Hence, in all cases, our conclusion is:
x > z

Ex. 8: Now, consider the combined inequality
x ≥ y ≥ z.
Here, x is either greater than y or equal to y. When x is greater than y we have: x > y ≥ z which gives the conclusion x > z... (a) [see Ex. 7]
When x is equal to y; we have:
x = y ≥ z which gives the conclusion x ≥ z ... (b)
Combining conclusion (a) and (b), we have
x ≥ z
What lessons do we learn from Ex.5, 6, 7 and 8? It is pretty obvious. The lesson we learn is our Golden Rule # 3.

Third Golden rule (for deriving a conclusion from a combined inequality)
The conclusion-inequality will have an '>' sign (or a '≥' sign) if (and only if) both the signs in the combined inequality were '>' (or '≥', as the case may be).
To understand this golden rule, look at examples 5, 6, 7, and 8 given above and the examples below:
Ex. 5: x > y > z
Ex. 6: x ≥ y ≥ z
